tests.intent: All motion speeds, waits, trigger range, damage cadence, and synchronized lighting durations are fixed reviewed engine definitions rather than caller-selected values.
The exact E1M1 linedef special set 1, 2, 11, 23, 26, 62, 88, and 117 is present, dispatched table-first, and no present action is silently ignored.
The exact E1M1 sector special set 1, 7, 9, and 12 is advanced every logical tic from relational sector state without client inference.
Special 1 is a front-side in-range repeatable manual normal door that opens, waits, closes, and can be activated again after a completed cycle.
Special 2 accepts only a front-to-back crossing, opens at normal door speed, stays open, and cannot retrigger after its one-shot activation.
Special 11 is an in-range front-side one-shot use action which records a trigger and deterministically transitions the map to completed state once.
Special 23 is an in-range front-side one-shot use action lowering every tagged floor at fixed speed to the independently derived lowest adjacent floor.
Special 26 denies a player without a blue key without consuming the repeatable trigger, emits denial, then opens normally after database-owned key acquisition.
Special 62 is an in-range repeatable switch lift with fixed lowering and raising speed, fixed bottom wait, and an exact 35-tic button texture reset.
Special 88 accepts front-to-back crossings only, performs the repeatable lower-wait-raise lift cycle, and has no use-button reset state.
Special 117 follows repeatable manual door rules but uses the reviewed blazing speed while retaining exact wait, closing, and obstruction behavior.
Use activation accepts the exact 64-unit boundary, rejects distances beyond it, and never trusts a caller-supplied linedef activation result.
Manual and switch activation rejects use from a linedef back side even when the candidate is otherwise closest and inside the exact use range.
Walkover actions distinguish front-to-back from back-to-front using exact signed geometry and reject tangency and endpoint-only non-crossings.
Once specials 2, 11, and 23 remain consumed while repeatable specials 1, 26, 62, 88, and 117 may activate on later valid interactions.
A repeatable switch changes database line state immediately, remains active for precisely 35 logical tics, then restores its reviewed texture and state once.
A closing raise-wait-close door encountering an occupied opening reverses safely to opening without crushing or deleting its active mover.
A lift blocked by an occupant at the start of raising preserves its floor and mover state until clear, then resumes at the fixed engine speed.
Sector special 7 applies exactly five damage on each 32nd exposure tic, no earlier, uses current player occupancy, and clamps health deterministically.
Sector special 9 increments the session player secret count and emits discovery exactly once even across exit, revisit, and combined interactions.
Sector special 12 derives synchronized five-tic bright and 35-tic dark phases from logical map tic, independent of insertion order or wall time.
Sector special 1 consumes the database Doom random table in stable order and applies reviewed masked bright and dark durations without host randomness.
A trigger applies to every and only sector carrying its relational tag with stable sector ordering and without embedding E1M1 linedef identifiers.
One independent focused replay exists for every required line and sector special and checks intermediate machine states rather than final state alone.
Door, lift, button, damage, light, secret, and exit interactions compose over many tics with stable event ordinals and no machine starving another.
All world mutation remains session-scoped inside its owning tic transaction, propagates errors, performs no commit, and cannot affect another session.
Production is audited for relational definitions and set-oriented machines while rejecting evaluator reads, fixture answers, host inspection, dynamic SQL, and autonomous commits.
Each isolated semantic defect has an independent focused witness covering triggers, timing, occupancy, hazards, lighting, secrets, completion, and relational targeting.
